title: Order-Disorder Tricriticality in $\mathrm{A}_n \mathrm{B}_n$ Star Polymer Melts
authors: Minhoon Kim, Wonjun Kang, Daeseong Yong, Junhan Cho, Jaeup U. Kim
abstract: Tricriticality usually requires tuning an additional thermodynamic parameter. Here we show that, in symmetric $\mathrm{A}_n\mathrm{B}_n$ star-polymer melts, the arm number $n$ itself plays this role and drives the order--disorder transition (ODT) from second order to first order. By developing a sixth-order free-energy expansion within the random phase approximation and comparing it with self-consistent field theory (SCFT) calculations, we analytically identify a tricritical arm number, $n_{\mathrm{tc}}\approx 5.4475$. For $n<n_{\mathrm{tc}}$, the lamellar ordering transition remains continuous and occurs at the spinodal point, $(χN)_{\mathrm{s}}\approx 10.495$. For $n>n_{\mathrm{tc}}$, the transition becomes first order, and $(χN)_{\mathrm{ODT}}$ shifts below $(χN)_{\mathrm{s}}$ with a quadratic dependence near the tricritical point. SCFT calculations confirm the predicted transition character and phase-boundary shift. The origin of this behavior is traced to inter-arm correlations generated by the common junction. We further show that the noninteger tricritical arm number can be effectively realized in binary mixtures of star polymers. This provides a rare analytically tractable example of architecture-induced tricriticality in a microphase-separating polymer system.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the architecture of a star polymer, specifically the number of arms, can act as a thermodynamic tuning parameter to induce tricriticality. By finding a specific arm number where the order-disorder transition changes from continuous to first-order, the study provides a rare analytically tractable example of architecture-induced phase transitions.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of the order-disorder transition (ODT) in symmetric star polymer melts and identifies the mechanism that drives the transition from second-order to first-order.
main_result: The authors analytically identify a tricritical arm number, n_tc approximately 5.4475, where the transition character changes, and show that the transition is driven by inter-arm correlations at the common junction.
method: The researchers use a sixth-order free-energy expansion within the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) and validate the results using Self-Consistent Field Theory (SCFT) and Langevin Field Theory simulations.
model_or_system: The system consists of incompressible melts of symmetric A_nB_n star polymers, where each star has n arms of type A and n arms of type B connected to a common junction, modeled using a Gaussian chain model.
key_observables: Order parameter (density modulation amplitude), phase boundary shift, and the quartic and sextic Landau coefficients.
important_parameters: Arm number (n), Flory-Huggins interaction parameter (chi*N), and composition fraction (f).
main_assumptions: The model assumes incompressible melts and uses a mean-field framework (RPA and SCFT) within a Gaussian chain approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows free-energy curves illustrating continuous vs. discontinuous transitions; Figure 2 shows the vanishing and sign change of the quartic coefficient at the tricritical point.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of transition character, develops a sixth-order RPA expansion, provides analytical predictions for the tricritical point and phase boundaries, validates these with SCFT, and discusses the physical origin via inter-arm correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase-transition behavior of symmetric AnBn star polymer systems: (a) Second-order transitions for n ≤5. (b) First-order transitions for n ≥6. The vertical gray line marks (χN)s ≈10.495.
FIG. 2. Coefficients of F as functions of n: (a) The quartic coefficient f4 vanishes and changes sign at n = ntc ≈5.4475. (b) The sextic coefficient f6 remains positive in the vicinity of n = ntc.
FIG. 3. Comparison of the sixth-order RPA prediction for the phase boundary with the SCFT results.
